themes.name: Rabbit Holes Worth Falling Down
themes.tagline: Big systems explained beautifully — from star maps to border disputes to the pizza empires that fed America.

items.title: Mythology Expert Breaks Down Every Ancient Greek Constellation
items.description:
Mythology expert and Professor of classics Peter Meineck deep dives into all 48 of the constellations the ancient Greeks used in the night sky. From maritime navigation to agriculture and timekeeping, Meineck explains how eight main groupings of constellations were tracked in the night sky—and how they translate to today’s mythological storytelling.

WIRED recommends:
https://youtu.be/GkM5zdUHcaQ
https://youtu.be/p8qElr8nWzQ
https://youtu.be/o-jUQhCeazQ

What do Iron Man and Odysseus have in com
items.why_watch: Professor Peter Meineck treats the 48 Greek constellations not as trivia but as a working system — one the ancients used for navigation, farming, and timekeeping. The organizing framework of eight groupings gives a genuinely new way to see the night sky rather than a random mythology dump. It's an hour well spent if you've ever looked up and felt like you were missing context.

items.title: How Every Major Pizza Chain Came To Be | Compilation
items.description:
Pizza.... we have all had it, we all have our favorite toppings. It's even been said, 'even when it's bad, it's still pretty good!' Pizza has a very long and storied past... from the ancient days up to the point you can get a nice hot pie in 30 minutes or less delivered to your door! Which is your favorite pizza? What about your favorite toppings? Let us know in the comments!

Chapters:

00:00:00 - $1 Pizza Vs. $1,000 Pizza
00:12:13 - The Greasy Downfall Of Sbarro Pizza
00:21:33 - Everything Tha
items.why_watch: Nearly two hours of the actual origin stories behind Domino's, Pizza Hut, Little Caesars, and others — less food tourism, more business history told through immigrant ambition and postwar American appetite. Good background listening if you enjoy the 'how did this become everywhere' genre of corporate storytelling.

items.title: India vs China, Explained
items.description:
Inside the Rivalry Between Asia’s Two Giants
Take back your personal data with Incogni! Use code johnnyharris at the link below and get 60% off annual plans: https://incogni.com/johnnyharris 

Check out all my sources for this video here: https://docs.google.com/document/d/1liPrhZ4_-_mwJ_xpg1os-8R1qxsPPt0Qn_LGZ6N9sOM/edit?usp=sharing

India and China share the world’s largest disputed border, and their rivalry with global consequences. Through the lenses of the border, trade, the Indian Ocean, a
items.why_watch: Johnny Harris maps the India-China rivalry through its least-understood dimension: the 2,100-mile disputed border and the military infrastructure both sides have been quietly building along it. He grounds the geopolitics in specific geography and recent flashpoints, making a genuinely complex relationship legible without oversimplifying it.

items.title: Edinburgh - 1700s
items.description: Step into Edinburgh before the New Town, when the city was dense, defensive, and shaped by centuries of pressure. This video explores 18th-century Edinburgh through its streets, landmarks, and hidden edges — from the castle rock and crowded High Street to vanished features like the Nor Loch. Using modern AI tools and historic paintings, maps, and engravings, the past is reconstructed in motion, allowing us to move through these scenes and experience the city as it once was. Stay until the end to
items.why_watch: Matt Reconstructs History rebuilds 1700s Edinburgh — before the planned New Town was built — using AI visuals layered against period maps, paintings, and engravings, giving you a real sense of how dense and vertical the old city actually was. The Nor Loch, the crowded High Street, the logic of building upward on a volcanic ridge — it all clicks into place in under 25 minutes.

themes.name: The Examined Life
themes.tagline: Philosophy, communication, and the art of thinking clearly — for people who argue with books in the margins.

items.title: How to define the conversation | Charles Duhigg
items.description: How to define the conversation | Charles Duhigg
items.why_watch: Charles Duhigg distills a single, underused communication skill: deciding what kind of conversation you're actually in before it goes sideways. At under three minutes, it's the kind of reframe that makes you immediately rethink your last difficult exchange. Practical and precise.

items.title: Ranking Classic Philosophy Books (with Joe Folley)
items.description:
I invited Joe Folley (from  @unsolicitedadvice9198) to help me make a tier list of classic works of philosophy.

My article on Stoicism: https://jaredhenderson.substack.com/p/why-i-am-not-a-modern-stoic

Watch my videos early on Nebula: https://go.nebula.tv/jaredhenderson

→ Chapters

00:00 Beginning
01:57 The Republic
19:08 Nicomachean Ethics
36:24 Meditations
56:04 Confessions
1:05:58 Consolations of Philosophy
1:12:23 Summa Theologica
1:21:43 Montaigne's Essays
1:29:09 Meditations
1:41:27 Lev
items.why_watch: Jared Henderson and Joe Folley work through canonical philosophy texts — Plato, Aristotle, Marcus Aurelius, Augustine — arguing seriously about which ones still hold up and which are more monument than reading experience. The tier-list format keeps a four-hour runtime moving, and the disagreements are substantive enough to make you want to revisit books you thought you knew.

items.title: A FULL BREAKDOWN of ALL Symbols, Themes, and Social Commentary in Pluribus
items.description: In this Apple TV Pluribus Explained / Pluribus Season 1 Ending Explained and Analysis, I deep-dive analyze the Pluribus series, starring Rhea Seehorn, Karolina Wydra, Carlos Manuel Vesga, Miriam Shor, and Samba Schutte. Created by Vince Gilligan, the series is a perfect blend of sci-fi horror and psychological thriller, exploring themes of happiness, peace, and identity. I believe this film is excellent in capturing the major themes of social conformity and individuality through great writing, a
items.why_watch: Vince Gilligan's Apple TV+ series 'Pluribus' rewards close reading, and Lucas Blue does the work of mapping its symbols and social commentary systematically rather than impressionistically. If you've finished the show and felt there was more under the surface, this is the breakdown that will confirm or sharpen your instincts.

themes.name: Slow & Rooted
themes.tagline: Dirt under the fingernails, snow in the Alps, and cities that no longer exist — a theme for slowing all the way down.

items.why_watch: GrowVeg's Ben tests whether you can start a productive garden with literally no money, leaning on cuttings, seed-saving, and supermarket herb rescues. The constraints make it more instructive than a standard gardening tutorial — you see which shortcuts actually work. Fourteen minutes with real, replicable results.

If you follow them for the lifestyle as much as the construction, this is a warm detour worth the 16 minutes.

global_silver_linings.title: Homegrown Catnip Lotion Proves to Be An Effective Mosquito Repellent in Rural Uganda
global_silver_linings.snippet: Homegrown catnip plants have proven to be as effective as DEET for repelling mosquitoes in rural Uganda. Presented at an international science conference in Florence, Italy, a collaborative team of Welsh and Ugandan researchers believe they’ve found a lower cost solution to mosquito control that can reduce reliance on malaria medication, which the parasite is […] The post Homegrown Catnip Lotion Proves to Be An Effective Mosquito Repellent in Rural Uganda appeared first on Good News Network .
global_silver_linings.reason: A plant anyone can grow in their garden is proving to repel mosquitoes as effectively as synthetic chemicals, offering rural communities in Uganda an affordable, homegrown shield against disease. It's a beautiful reminder that nature often holds practical solutions we've barely begun to explore.

global_silver_linings.snippet: "I made it happen through determination, a very tight schedule, and many nights of running on five hours of sleep or less." The post In just 15 days, this Ohio woman crocheted her own wedding dress and the wedding party’s attire appeared first on Upworthy .
global_silver_linings.reason: One woman's decision to crochet her entire wedding wardrobe in 15 days is a joyful testament to what human hands and determination can create outside the pressures of consumer culture. There's something quietly radical about choosing craft over convenience for one of life's biggest moments.

Ten solutions this week with Arielle and Karissa, including the […] The post Podcast Transcript July 17th, 2026— Wildfire satellites, Seine city cooling, and parenting securely attached kids first appeared on The Optimist Daily: Making Solutions the News .
global_silver_linings.reason: From satellites tracking wildfires in real time to a major city harnessing its river to cool urban streets, these emerging tools show that communities are finding genuinely creative ways to adapt and protect the places we love.

discovery_articles.title: The Solgrylle at the Blacksmith's Bridge in Aarhus, Denmark
discovery_articles.snippet: Smedens Bro , "The Blacksmith's Bridge", is a pedestrian and cycling bridge in central Aarhus. It was built in 1911 at the initiative of the local ironsmith and businessman Hans Peter Jensen, who funded its construction to improve access for residents of his neighbourhood. Spanning the Aarhus River, the light grey railings rise and curve outward at both ends, framing the city's coat of arms.

discovery_articles.title: Borden Avenue Bridge in Queens, New York
discovery_articles.snippet: New York City is a city of the water, built on a series of islands criss-crossed by rivers and streams. So the city is also populated by almost 800 bridges, from giant spans like the Verrazzano to smaller wooden trestles that might only cross a tiny creek. In the case of the Borden Avenue Bridge, this doesn’t mean it’s boring or unimpressive.

discovery_articles.title: Grace Episcopal Church of City Island in Bronx, New York
discovery_articles.snippet: Many New Yorkers will be familiar with Manhattan’s Grace Church, a French Gothic masterpiece built in 1846–1847 on Broadway. Or, if they’re from Brooklyn, perhaps they’re more acquainted with the Grace Church on Hicks Street, which was built in 1847 when the other Grace Church’s then-new location proved too far a trip for some faithful. However, for parishioners in the Bronx, specifically in the small seafaring community of City Point, Grace Episcopal Church on City Island Avenue is the one true bearer of the popular name – and this National Historic Place just so happens to have been built around the same time as its two more famous namesakes, in 1849.

discovery_articles.title: Michael Jackson HIStory Statue in Villan-Södra Utmarken, Sweden
discovery_articles.snippet: [ NOTE TO THE EDITOR: If this entry is published, please correct the geo header by replacing 'Villan-Södra Utmarken' (a neighborhood) with ' Ängelholm '.] Additionally, the Ängelholm UFO Memorial , which is already published, has the redundant compass location "V" (Väst / West) after the city name in the geo header. By the way, perhaps my submission, if accepted, and the other location would warrant an Ängelholm destination guide? +  +  + In 1995, Sony Music commissioned ten identical 33-foot (10-meter) fiberglass statues of pop icon Michael Jackson to promote the album HIStory: Past, Present and Future, Book I .

discovery_articles.snippet: The vessel "Quest," found in 2024 off the coast of northeastern Canada, has become a home for corals, anemones and fish. It also holds the leftovers from decades of commercial fishing that happened above it after it sank

featured_read.title: Marcus Aurelius on the Good Luck of Your Bad Luck: The Stoic Strategy for Weathering Life’s Waves and Turning Suffering into Strength
featured_read.snippet: "What happened could have happened to anyone, but not everyone could have carried on."
featured_read.blurb: Marcus Aurelius offers something rarer than consolation: the argument that the specific shape of your hardship is, quietly, a measure of your capacity to bear it. A Stoic reframe so precise it almost stings — in the best way.

garden_note.note: First light crept over the Zürich rooftops at 05:09 this morning, and the garden is already humming — high summer here means the courgettes have doubled overnight and the lavender is at its fragrant, bee-loud peak. Step outside before the heat builds and you'll catch the best of it.
in_season: Lavender — harvest flower spikes before midday heat fades the oils
Courgettes — check daily and pick small for best flavour
Climbing French beans — should be podding well now
Deadhead roses to encourage a late-summer flush
garden_note.sky_tonight: A slender waxing crescent moon hangs low in the western sky after dusk — a lovely sight above the Albis hills around 22:00 before it follows the sun down.
garden_note.sun_times: First light arrives at 05:09, the sun rises at 05:46, and the long Swiss summer day closes gently with dusk at 21:54.

puzzle.label: Fern's Morning Anagram
puzzle.prompt: Rearrange the letters of LISTEN to make a different common word.
puzzle.hint: There are 4 words hiding in these letters.

previous_puzzle.label: The Evening Almanac
previous_puzzle.prompt:
A summer evening puzzle for the patient and curious. Three true things happened in the same year — name it within a decade:

A Scottish engineer demonstrated the first working television picture, transmitting the flickering image of a ventriloquist's dummy in a London attic.

A young American novelist published a story about a man who cannot escape his own hollow glamour, set against the Long Island summer heat.

Britain's coal miners walked out in the largest general strike the country had ever seen, halting trains and newspapers alike for nine days.

What year do these belong to?
previous_puzzle.answer: 1926. John Logie Baird demonstrated television in January of that year; F. Scott Fitzgerald published The Great Gatsby in 1925 — correction: the novel clue should be set aside. The confirmed year is 1926: Baird's television demonstration and the British General Strike are both firmly placed there.

fern_data.greeting: While the bread is still warm and the coffee earns its keep, settle in — today's canopy stretches from the Greek stars overhead to the philosophy of how we even talk to each other, with enough rabbit holes to keep you gloriously lost until lunch.
